content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Asistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660          322 5660                322 5901           322 6441          322 6444

June 3, 1980


Sidney Maleck
City Attorney
P.O. Box 6008
El Monte, CA  91734

Dear Sid:

     This is in response to your letter dated May 19, 1980,
on behalf of Councilman Dan McMillen and Jack Crippen, the
Mayor of El Monte, concerning the following situation.
There is a local chapter of the Boys' Club to which the City
Council of El Monte provides an annual grant of $250.
Mayor Crippen and Councilman McMillen are members of the
chapter's Board of Directors.  You are concerned about the
extent to which these officials may participate in Council
decisions to award the grant to the organization.

     The Political Reform Act requires public officials to
refrain from participating in governmental decisions in
which they have a financial interest.  See Government Code
Section 87100.  The Act clarifies the meaning of "financial
interest" in Government Code Section 87103 which provides:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

                         . . .

     (c)  Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard
to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within twelve months
prior to the time wh n the decision is made; or

Sidney Maleck
June 3, 1980
Page Two


(d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management....

(Emphasis added.)

It is not disputed that a $25,000 grant to the local chapter
of the Boys' Club will have a material impact on the organization.
Therefore, the Political Reform Act requires the officials
to refrain from participating in the Council vote to provide
the grant if the Club is a source of income to the officials
or a business entity of which they are directors.

You have indicated to me that neither of the officials
in question has received income from the Boys' Club. Furthermore,
the Club is not a business entity as defined by the Political
Reform Act because it is a non-profit organization (see
Government Code Section 82005). For these reasons it would
not be a violation of Government Code Sections 87100 and
87103 for the officials to participate in a Council decision
to provide a $25,000 grant to the local Boys' Club.

This advice interpreting the Political Reform Act is
provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b), but
it should be noted that the Fair Political Practices Commission
does not have jurisdiction over other laws pertaining to
conflicts of interests. If there are other laws that would
disqualify the officials from participating in the decision
to award the grant or nullify their votes, the Political
Reform Act would not prohibit those laws from taking effect.

I hope that this letter is helpful. If I can be of
further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Dwight E. Dickerson
Counsel
Legal Division

DED:plh

. . . . .

CITY OF ELMONTE

CITY HALL
TELEPHONE (213) 575-2250
EL MONTE, CALIFORNIA 91734

MAY 21 - 9.07 AM 80
3050 N. EVERVARD

CITY ATTORNEY
SIDNEY MALECK

DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY
M. R. WARD, JR.

May 19, 1980


Mr. Dwight Dickerson
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Dwight:

Please be advised that at the last election a gentleman was elected
to the City Council who holds the position of President of our local
Boys Club. In addition, our mayor is on the Board of Directors.

The Council has been supporting the Boys Club with an annual $25,000
grant. Since this amount must be voted each year, the question that
I have is whether the new councilman and the mayor will be permitted
to vote on this matter. The Boys Club is a non-profit, community
organization.

In reviewing the Conflict of Interest pamphlet distributed by the
Attorney General, it appears to me that they have remote interests
and would be allowed to vote, but the vote could not be counted and
therefore, as a practical matter, they should not vote. However, I
would like to know if there is an FPPC opinion which would hold
otherwise.

Yours truly,

Sidney Maleck
City Attorney

SM:evn

Visit Friendly El Monte

There is a local chapter of the Boys' Club to which the City Council of El Monte provides an annual grant of $25,000. Mayor Crippen and Councilman McMillen are members of the chapter's Board of Directors. You are concerned about the extent to which these officials may participate in Council decisions to award the grant to the organization. The Political Reform Act requires public officials to refrain from participating in governmental decisions in which they have a financial interest. See Government Code Section 87100. The Act clarifies the meaning of "financial interest" in Government Code Section 87103 which provides: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or (d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.... (Emphasis added.) It is not disputed that a $25,000 grant to the local chapter of the Boys' Club will have a material impact on the organization. Therefore, the Political Reform Act requires the officials to refrain from participating in the Council vote to provide the grant if the Club is a source of income to the officials or a business entity of which they are directors. You have indicated to me that neither of the officials in question has received income from the Boys' Club. Furthermore, the Club is not a business entity as defined by the Political Reform Act because it is a non-profit organization (see Government Code Section 82005). For these reasons it would not be a violation of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 for the officials to participate in a Council decision to provide a $25,000 grant to the local Boys' Club.
